2604.24026.title: From Skill Text to Skill Structure: The Scheduling-Structural-Logical Representation for Agent Skills
2604.24026.paper_summary: LLM agents increasingly rely on reusable skills, capability packages that combine instructions, control flow, constraints, and tool calls. In most current agent systems, however, skills are still represented by text-heavy artifacts, including SKILL.md-style documents and structured records whose machine-usable evidence remains embedded largely in natural-language descriptions. This poses a challenge for skill-centered agent systems: managing skill collections and using skills to support agent both require reasoning over invocation interfaces, execution structure, and concrete side effects that are often entangled in a single textual surface. An explicit representation of skill knowledge may therefore help make these artifacts easier for machines to acquire and leverage. Drawing on Memory Organization Packets, Script Theory, and Conceptual Dependency from Schank and Abelson's classical work on linguistic knowledge representation, we introduce what is, to our knowledge, the first structured representation for agent skill artifacts that disentangles skill-level scheduling signals, scene-level execution structure, and logic-level action and resource-use evidence: the Scheduling-Structural-Logical (SSL) representation. We instantiate SSL with an LLM-based normalizer and evaluate it on a corpus of skills in two tasks, Skill Discovery and Risk Assessment, and superiorly outperform the text-only baselines: in Skill Discovery, SSL improves MRR from 0.573 to 0.707; in Risk Assessment, it improves macro F1 from 0.744 to 0.787. These findings reveal that explicit, source-grounded structure makes agent skills easier to search and review. They also suggest that SSL is best understood as a practical step toward more inspectable, reusable, and operationally actionable skill representations for agent systems, rather than as a finished standard or an end-to-end mechanism for managing and using skills.
2604.24026.paper_summary_zh: LLM 代理越來越依賴可重用的技能，這些能力包結合了指令、控制流程、約束和工具調用。 然而，在大多數當前的代理系統中，技能仍然是由文本密集型的文檔表示，包括 SKILL.md 風格的文件和結構化記錄，其機器可用的證據仍然主要嵌入在自然語言描述中。 這對以技能為中心的代理系統提出了挑戰：管理技能集合和使用技能來支持代理都需要對調用介面、執行結構和具體副作用進行推理，而這些通常糾纏在單一的文本表面中。 因此，對技能知識的明確表示可能有助於使這些文檔更容易被機器獲取和利用。 我們借鑒了記憶組織包、腳本理論以及 Schank 和 Abelson 在語言知識表示方面的經典研究中的概念依賴，介紹了我們所知的第一個結構化表示，用於代理技能文檔，該表示將技能級調度信號、場景級執行結構和邏輯級動作及資源使用證據解開：調度-結構-邏輯 (SSL) 表示。 我們用基於 LLM 的正規化器實現 SSL，並在兩個任務的技能語料庫上進行評估，即技能發現和風險評估，並優於僅文本基準：在技能發現中，SSL 將 MRR 從 0.573 提高到 0.707；在風險評估中，將宏觀 F1 從 0.744 提高到 0.787。 這些發現揭示了明確的、源基礎的結構使代理技能更容易搜索和審查。 它們還表明，SSL 最好被理解為朝著更可檢查、可重用和可操作的代理系統技能表示邁出的實際一步，而不是作為一個完成的標準或一個端到端的機制來管理和使用技能。
2604.24026.author: Qiliang Liang et.al.
2604.24026.authors: Qiliang Liang, Hansi Wang, Zhong Liang, Yang Liu
